anvaya.meaning: in whom rajas has been calmed

translations.verbatim_quote: The yogī whose mind is fixed on Me verily attains the highest perfection of transcendental happiness. He is beyond the mode of passion, he realizes his qualitative identity with the Supreme, and thus he is freed from all reactions to past deeds.

The yogi whose mind is fixed on Me verily attains the highest perfection of transcendental happiness. He is beyond the mode of passion, he realizes his qualitative identity with the Supreme, and thus he is freed from all reactions to past deeds..

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 507 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Brahma-bhūta is the state of being free from material contamination and situated in the transcendental service of the Lord. Mad-bhaktiṁ labhate parām (Bg. 18.54). One cannot remain in the quality of Brahman, the Absolute, until one’s mind is fixed on the lotus feet of the Lord. Sa vai manaḥ kṛṣṇa-padāravindayoḥ.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

positions.rendering: yogī whose mind is fixed on Me ... whose passion is pacified, who is freed from all sin
rajas (cause of attachment and aversion) has been calmed; become Brahman, free of every form of stain
the mind rendered free of rajas (the source of activity) and fixed entirely on the Self

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All traditions agree that śānta-rajasam names the calming of the rajo-guna specifically. Shankara reads rajas as the cause of attachment-aversion; Ramanuja reads rajas as the source of mental activity. Both identify the calming as the precondition for the supreme happiness named. The doctrinal point is the same.

literal_meaning: Indeed, supreme happiness comes to this yogi of peaceful mind, in whom rajas (restlessness) has been calmed, who has become Brahman, free from stain.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.27 names the result-state of the practice. The yogi praśānta-manas (peaceful-minded) and śānta-rajas (in whom the rajas-guna has been calmed) attains sukham uttamam (supreme happiness). The condition: brahma-bhūtam (one who has become Brahman) and akalmaṣam (free of stain). Shankara: rajas is the cause of attachment and aversion; when calmed, the yogi has become Brahman, free of every form of stain. Ramanuja: when the mind has been rendered free of rajas (the source of activity), and is fixed entirely on the Self, supreme happiness arises naturally. Per chapter-6 thesis, this is the rajas-calmed state — the engineer with restless effort settled is not slow but settled-in-action. STRETCHED at metaphysical scope; partial engineering analog at calmed-effort scope.
